original_reasoning: حضا is stirring up and making fire blaze; Greek abundant or poured forth fits a flaring, overflowing result.
Arabic 'pierce, break through' can extend to entering into an act; Greek 'to attempt' preserves that deeper sense of pushing into an undertaking.
Greek is a linen garment; Arabic is a thin wafer-like sheet, so both meet in the core of thin spread textile-like layering.
Arabic 'raise, lift up' can meet Greek 'cover with a roof' through the act of putting something up over a structure.
حزا includes driving, collecting, and managing movement; that can plausibly invert into giving way or falling back as the other side of directed motion.
To make clear/reveal and the Greek sense of brightness/gleam can meet through a shared notion of manifest visibility.
نصب=to erect/set up; Νίσιβις=Nisibis (city whose name meant 'pillars' in Semitic). Possible Semitic etymology link

Arabic damar = enter without permission; al-damar = ruin, destruction. Greek damar = wife, spouse (from damao 'tame, subdue'). The 'subdue/take/enter' core is shared — and Arabic d-m-r vs Greek damar are phonetically near-identical. The 'taming a wife' or 'entering' bridge is plausible across both Semitic and PIE damage/conquer fields. Strong combined link.
